title: Tenses — Band 7+ Reference
subtitle: All 12 tenses + reported speech + the Band 6→7 transition errors
intro: Tenses are the most-noticed grammar feature in IELTS. Every clause has a verb; every wrong tense is a visible error. Band 6 candidates control 4–5 tenses fluently. Band 7+ candidates control all 12 with intentional choice. This reference shows the form, the uses, the IELTS context, and — critically — the mistakes that pull a Band 7 candidate down to 6.5.

sections.title: 1. The 12 Tenses — One-Page Map
sections.summary: English has 4 aspects (Simple, Continuous, Perfect, Perfect Continuous) × 3 times (Past, Present, Future) = 12 tenses. Memorise the form of each, then learn when to use it.

rules.pattern: PRESENT — 4 forms

examples.plural: I work / She works
I am working / She is working
I have worked / She has worked
I have been working / She has been working

rules.pattern: PAST — 4 forms

examples.plural: I worked / She worked
I was working / She was working
I had worked / She had worked
I had been working / She had been working

rules.pattern: FUTURE — 4 forms (plus 2 alternative future expressions)

examples.plural: I will work / She will work
I will be working / She will be working
I will have worked / She will have worked
I will have been working / She will have been working
I am going to work
I am working tomorrow

rules.note: Band 7+ writers use at least 8 of these naturally across a 250-word essay. If yours uses only 3–4, that's the first thing to fix.

sections.tip: Print this map on one page and stick it on your desk for the first week of practice.

sections.title: 2. Present Tenses — Forms, Uses, Signals
sections.summary: Each present tense has a specific function. Choosing the wrong one is the most common Band 6 grammar error.

rules.pattern: PRESENT SIMPLE — form: V / V-s (3rd person sing.)

examples.plural: I drink tea every morning.
Water boils at 100°C.
The train leaves at 6 a.m.
She lives in Dhaka.

items: Signal words: always, often, usually, sometimes, never, every day, on Mondays, generally
rules.note: Common mistake: forgetting the -s on third person singular ('She go' instead of 'She goes'). This single error appears in almost every Band 6 essay.

rules.pattern: PRESENT CONTINUOUS — form: am/is/are + V-ing

examples.plural: I am writing a paragraph.
She is staying with her sister this month.
More people are working from home these days.
We are meeting the client at 3 p.m. tomorrow.

items: Signal words: now, at the moment, currently, these days, this week/month/year, today
rules.note: Do NOT use with stative verbs ('I am knowing the answer' is wrong — say 'I know the answer'). See the Stative Verbs section.

rules.pattern: PRESENT PERFECT — form: have/has + V3 (past participle)

examples.plural: I have lost my keys (and still don't have them).
She has visited five countries.
We have lived here since 2020 / for five years.
I have just finished my essay.

items: Signal words: ever, never, just, already, yet, since, for, recently, lately, so far, up to now, in the last few years
rules.note: The single highest-yield tense for IELTS. See section 6 for the critical Present Perfect vs Past Simple contrast.

rules.pattern: PRESENT PERFECT CONTINUOUS — form: have/has been + V-ing

examples.singular: action started in past, still continuing
examples.plural: I have been studying English for ten years.
She is tired because she has been running.
We have been meeting every week to plan the project.

items: Signal words: for, since, all day, all week, lately, recently, how long
rules.note: Difference from Present Perfect: Continuous emphasises duration/process; Simple emphasises completion. 'I have written 3 essays' (count, complete) vs 'I have been writing essays all morning' (process, ongoing).

sections.title: 3. Past Tenses — Forms, Uses, Narrative Flow
sections.summary: Past tenses tell stories. Band 7+ candidates layer all four — Simple for the main events, Continuous for background, Perfect for earlier-than-the-story actions, Perfect Continuous for ongoing background actions.

rules.pattern: PAST SIMPLE — form: V2 (regular: V+ed; irregular: memorise)

examples.plural: I visited Paris in 2018.
She walked to school every day as a child.
He opened the door, looked inside, and left.
We were happy in those days.

items: Signal words: yesterday, last week/month/year, in 2010, ago, when I was young, then
rules.note: If a finished time is mentioned (yesterday, in 2010, last year, when I was a child), Past Simple is required — NOT Present Perfect. ❌ 'I have visited Paris in 2018.' ✔ 'I visited Paris in 2018.'

rules.pattern: PAST CONTINUOUS — form: was/were + V-ing

examples.singular: action in progress at a past moment
examples.plural: At 8 p.m. last night I was watching TV.

examples.singular: background to a Past Simple event
examples.plural: I was cooking when the phone rang.
While I was reading, my brother was playing music.
He was always interrupting me.

items: Signal words: while, as, when, at 5 o'clock yesterday, all day yesterday

rules.pattern: PAST PERFECT — form: had + V3

examples.singular: an action completed BEFORE another past action
examples.plural: By the time I arrived, the meeting had finished.

examples.singular: to give background that pre-dates the story
examples.plural: She had studied medicine before becoming a writer.
If I had known, I would have helped.

items: Signal words: by the time, before, after, already, just, when (in 'when I arrived, they had left')
rules.note: Don't use Past Perfect for every past event in a story — only when one past event clearly happened BEFORE another. Overuse sounds awkward.

rules.pattern: PAST PERFECT CONTINUOUS — form: had been + V-ing

examples.singular: action that continued up to a point in the past
examples.plural: She had been waiting for an hour when the bus finally arrived.
His eyes were red because he had been crying.

items: Signal words: for, since, all day, before, until then

sections.tip: Speaking Part 2 cue cards reward narrative tense layering. Plan a 2-minute answer where you use Past Simple + Past Continuous + Past Perfect in the same answer — that signals Band 7+ grammatical range.

sections.title: 4. Future — 6 Ways to Talk About the Future
sections.summary: Band 7+ writers don't only use 'will'. There are six ways, each with a different shade of meaning.

rules.pattern: WILL — predictions, instant decisions, promises

examples.plural: I think it will rain tomorrow.
OK, I'll have the chicken.
I will help you with the assignment.

rules.pattern: BE GOING TO — intentions and evidence-based predictions

examples.plural: I am going to study medicine next year.
Look at those clouds — it is going to rain.

rules.note: If you can SEE evidence, prefer 'going to' over 'will'.

rules.pattern: PRESENT CONTINUOUS — fixed future arrangements

examples.plural: I am flying to London on Friday.
We are meeting the client at 3 p.m. tomorrow.

rules.pattern: PRESENT SIMPLE — timetables / scheduled events

examples.plural: The train leaves at 6 a.m.
Term starts on the first of October.

rules.pattern: FUTURE CONTINUOUS — action in progress at a future point

examples.plural: This time next year, I will be living in Sydney.
Will you be using the car tonight?

rules.pattern: FUTURE PERFECT — action completed by a future point

examples.plural: By 2050, automation will have replaced many jobs.
By next month, I will have lived here for ten years.

rules.note: Future Perfect is rare and high-value. One sentence with 'will have + V3' in a Task 2 conclusion about the future signals advanced grammar control.

sections.tip: Aim to use at least 3 of these 6 future forms in any 250-word Task 2 essay that discusses the future.

sections.title: 5. Stative Verbs — The 'I am loving it' Trap
sections.summary: Stative verbs describe states (not actions) and normally do NOT take continuous (-ing) forms. Saying 'I am knowing' or 'I am owning' instantly drops your perceived band.

rules.pattern: Common stative verbs — DO NOT use in continuous
items: Mental: know, believe, understand, think (= opinion), realise, recognise, remember, forget, doubt
Emotional: love, hate, like, dislike, prefer, want, need, wish, mind
Possession: have (= own), own, belong, possess, contain, consist of
Senses: see, hear, smell, taste, feel, notice, sound, look (= appear), seem, appear
Other: be, exist, depend (on), matter, deserve, mean, fit, weigh, owe, cost
rules.note: Use Simple form: ❌ 'I am knowing the answer.' ✔ 'I know the answer.' ❌ 'She is having a car.' ✔ 'She has a car.'

rules.pattern: Dual-use verbs — meaning shifts between Simple and Continuous

examples.plural: I'm thinking about the problem (= mental activity = dynamic)
She is having a shower / a meal (= experience = dynamic)

examples.singular: I see what you mean (= understand)
examples.plural: I am seeing the doctor at 3 (= meeting)

examples.singular: The food tastes good (= has flavour)
examples.plural: The chef is tasting the soup (= testing flavour)
He is being rude today (= temporary behaviour)

rules.note: When in doubt, default to the Simple form — it's almost always safer.

sections.tip: Make a personal list of 15 stative verbs you use most often, and drill saying them in Simple form under Speaking pressure. Under stress, candidates default to '-ing' — train against that habit.

sections.title: 6. Present Perfect vs Past Simple — IELTS-Critical Contrast
sections.summary: The single highest-yield tense distinction for IELTS. Hours of practice on this one rule pay off more than any other tense work.

rules.pattern: Past Simple = FINISHED time period (mentioned or implied)

examples.plural: I visited the museum yesterday.
She moved here in 2010.
We met five years ago.

rules.pattern: Present Perfect = UNFINISHED time period OR result-now

examples.plural: We have lived here since 2020 / for five years.
Have you ever visited Paris? / I have never tried sushi.

examples.singular: just / already / yet (recent + connected to now)
examples.plural: I have already finished my work.
She has lost her keys (and still doesn't have them).

rules.pattern: Side-by-side comparison

examples.singular: I lived in Dhaka for 5 years (no longer)
examples.plural: I have lived in Dhaka for 5 years (still do)

examples.singular: She visited Paris three times (in past, no longer relevant)
examples.plural: She has visited Paris three times (life-experience, up to now)

examples.singular: Did you finish? (specific past time understood)
examples.plural: Have you finished? (right now, status check)

examples.singular: Technology changed dramatically in the 2000s (finished period)
examples.plural: Technology has changed dramatically over the last decade (continues)

rules.pattern: IELTS Writing Task 1 — line graphs / trends

examples.plural: Production fell sharply between 2010 and 2015.
Energy consumption has risen steadily over the last decade.

rules.note: If the chart's last data point is the present (or near it), use Present Perfect. If it's clearly in the past, use Past Simple.

sections.tip: Open any Writing Task 2 introduction you've written. Find every 'happened / has happened / had happened' and check whether the time frame is finished, unfinished, or earlier-than-another-past. This single audit fixes more grammar errors than any other drill.

sections.title: 7. Narrative Tenses Together
sections.summary: Storytelling — in Speaking Part 2 and Writing Task 2 examples — needs all four past tenses layered. This is where Band 7+ candidates show range.

rules.pattern: The four-tense narrative pattern

examples.plural: main events (then I went home, she said hello)
background / setting (it was raining, people were waiting)
earlier-than-the-story (she had already left, I had never seen anything like it)
ongoing background up to the moment (I had been waiting for hours when the bus arrived)

rules.pattern: Example narrative paragraph (all four tenses in 3 sentences)

examples.plural: It was a Friday evening, and I had been working at my desk for several hours when the lights suddenly went out.
I stood up and walked to the window, but the entire street was in darkness.
I realised that the storm we had been warned about earlier in the week had finally arrived.

rules.note: This 3-sentence paragraph contains 4 past tenses. That is exactly what Band 7+ examiners look for.

sections.tip: Speaking Part 2 cue cards always invite past narration. Prepare 3 personal anecdotes (a difficult journey, a memorable meal, a time you were late) where you can deploy this 4-tense pattern.

sections.title: 8. Reported Speech — Backshift Rules
sections.summary: When the reporting verb is past (said, told, claimed), tenses in the reported clause normally shift one step back. Place / time references shift too. This is a frequent IELTS Speaking Part 3 area.

rules.pattern: Tense backshift (one step into the past)

examples.plural: 'I work here.' → He said he worked there.
'I am working.' → She said she was working.
'I have finished.' → He said he had finished.
'I went home.' → She said she had gone home.
'I will help.' → He said he would help.
'I can swim.' → She said she could swim.
'I may attend.' → He said he might attend.

rules.pattern: Place / time references also shift

examples.plural: 'I am tired now.' → She said she was tired then.
'I will leave today.' → He said he would leave that day.

examples.singular: tomorrow → the next day / the following day
examples.plural: 'I will see you tomorrow.' → She said she would see me the next day.

examples.singular: yesterday → the previous day / the day before
examples.plural: 'I called yesterday.' → He said he had called the day before.
'I live here.' → She said she lived there.
'I bought this book.' → He said he had bought that book.

rules.pattern: NO backshift when the statement is still true / a general truth / a future-still-pending

examples.plural: The teacher said the Earth orbits the Sun. (NOT 'orbited')
She said she lives in London. (still lives there)
He said he will fly tomorrow. (the flight hasn't happened yet)

rules.note: Backshift is the SAFE default. NOT-backshifting is a stylistic choice that examiners reward when used correctly.

rules.pattern: Reporting verbs beyond 'said' / 'told'
items: Stating: claim, declare, mention, point out, state, comment, remark, observe
Agreeing/disagreeing: agree, disagree, deny, refuse, dispute, contradict
Promising/threatening: promise, threaten, swear, vow, pledge
Asking: ask, enquire, wonder, want to know
rules.note: Using 'claimed' / 'argued' / 'pointed out' instead of 'said' is a vocabulary upgrade examiners notice. 'The author argued that…' is Band 7+; 'The author said that…' is Band 6.

sections.title: 9. Tenses in Conditionals & Time Clauses
sections.summary: After 'if', 'when', 'as soon as', 'before', 'after', 'until', 'unless' — DO NOT use 'will' in the subordinate clause, even when the meaning is future.

rules.pattern: After IF / WHEN / AS SOON AS / BEFORE / AFTER / UNTIL / UNLESS — use Present, not 'will'

examples.plural: If it rains tomorrow, we will cancel. (NOT 'If it will rain')
I will call you when I arrive. (NOT 'when I will arrive')
As soon as the meeting ends, we will leave. (NOT 'will end')
I will check the email before I leave the office.
Wait here until I get back.
Unless you study, you will fail. (NOT 'will study')

rules.note: This is among the top 3 grammar errors for Band 6 candidates. Drill it: the SUBORDINATE clause stays in Present; the MAIN clause gets the future.

rules.pattern: Conditionals — tense pattern at a glance

examples.plural: If water reaches 100°C, it boils. (Present + Present)
If it rains, we will stay home. (Present + will)
If I had time, I would help. (Past + would)
If I had known, I would have helped. (Past Perfect + would have)

rules.note: See the dedicated Conditionals reference for mixed conditionals, inversions, and IELTS Speaking Part 3 hypothetical questions.

sections.title: 10. Top Tense Errors at the Band 6 → Band 7+ Threshold
sections.summary: These are the specific tense mistakes that pull a Band 7 candidate down to Band 6.5 — and the fixes.

rules.pattern: 1. Using Past Simple where Present Perfect is needed

examples.singular: ❌ I lived here for five years.
examples.plural: ✔ I have lived here for five years. (still living here)
✔ Technology has changed a lot recently.

rules.pattern: 2. Using 'will' inside an if- or when-clause

examples.singular: ❌ If it will rain, we will stay home.
examples.plural: ✔ If it rains, we will stay home.

examples.singular: ❌ I will call when I will arrive.
examples.plural: ✔ I will call when I arrive.

rules.pattern: 3. Stative verbs in continuous form

examples.plural: ✔ I know the answer.

examples.singular: ❌ She is owning a flat in Dhaka.
examples.plural: ✔ She owns a flat in Dhaka.

rules.pattern: 4. Forgetting third-person -s in Present Simple

examples.singular: ❌ She go to school every day.
examples.plural: ✔ She goes to school every day.
✔ My country has many rivers.

rules.pattern: 5. Mixing tenses within a paragraph without reason

examples.singular: ❌ I went to the shop. I see my friend. We are talking for an hour.
examples.plural: ✔ I went to the shop. I saw my friend. We talked for an hour.

rules.note: Pick a tense for the timeline of your paragraph and stay in it unless the meaning changes.

rules.pattern: 6. Past Perfect for any past event (overuse)

examples.singular: ❌ Yesterday I had gone to the market and had bought some fruit.
examples.plural: ✔ Yesterday I went to the market and bought some fruit.

rules.note: Use Past Perfect only when one past event happened CLEARLY BEFORE another past event.

rules.pattern: 7. Wrong backshift in reported speech

examples.plural: ✔ He said he was tired.

examples.singular: ❌ She said she will come tomorrow.
examples.plural: ✔ She said she would come the next day. (or: She said she will come tomorrow — if tomorrow is still in the future at the time of writing)

rules.pattern: 8. Future tense for habitual present

examples.singular: ❌ Every weekend I will go to the market.
examples.plural: ✔ Every weekend I go to the market.

rules.pattern: 9. Using 'do/does/did' in question and forgetting in negative

examples.plural: ✔ I do not understand.
✔ Where do you live?
✔ She does not like coffee.

rules.pattern: 10. Using Present Continuous for permanent states

examples.singular: ❌ I am living in Dhaka all my life.
examples.plural: ✔ I have lived in Dhaka all my life.

examples.singular: ❌ My family is having three members.
examples.plural: ✔ My family has three members.

sections.tip: Print this list and run a verb-by-verb audit on every essay you write for the next 14 days. After two weeks of conscious checking, the corrections become automatic.

checklist: I can name and form all 12 tenses without hesitation.
I never forget the -s on third-person singular Present Simple ('She goes', 'It works').
I use Present Perfect (not Past Simple) for unfinished time periods (since/for) and life-experience (ever/never).
I use Past Simple (not Present Perfect) when a finished past time is mentioned (yesterday, in 2010, ago).
I never use 'will' inside an if- or when-clause that refers to the future.
I never put stative verbs (know, believe, own, love) into the -ing form.
I can layer Past Simple + Past Continuous + Past Perfect in one narrative paragraph.
I use at least 3 different future forms (will, going to, Present Continuous, Future Perfect) in a Task 2 essay.
I apply correct backshift in reported speech and recognise when not to backshift (general truths).
I use varied reporting verbs (claim, argue, point out, suggest) instead of repeating 'say'.
I can write a 3-sentence story that contains 4 different past tenses without it feeling forced.
I do NOT use Past Perfect for every past event — only when one event clearly pre-dates another.
